content:
# Deuteronomy 1 — Disagreement Log
**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 1 (46 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Total disagreements logged:** 22

---

## V.1 — Geographical and proper name forms

- **EMET:** "words that Moshe spoke to all Yisra'el beyond the Yarden...Chatserot and Di-zahav"
- **LOGOS:** "words that Moses spoke to all Israel beyond the Jordan...Hazeroth and Di-zahab"
- **PNEUMA:** "words Moses spoke to all Israel beyond the Jordan...Hazeroth, and Di-zahab"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** Conventional English forms throughout (Moses, Israel, Jordan, Hazeroth, Di-zahab) per project convention. EMET's Hebrew transliterations are rejected for running text.

---

## V.2 — "eleven days' journey"

- **EMET:** "eleven days from Horeb by the way of Mount Se'ir"
- **LOGOS:** "Eleven days' journey from Horeb by the way of Mount Seir"
- **PNEUMA:** "It is eleven days' journey from Horeb by way of Mount Seir"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "eleven days' journey" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew *derekh* (by the way of) supports the journey idiom; LOGOS/PNEUMA rendering is more natural.

---

## V.3 — "sons of Israel" vs. "children of Israel"

- **EMET:** "the sons of Yisra'el"
- **LOGOS:** "the children of Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "the sons of Israel"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "sons of Israel" — the project's established convention for *bney yisrael*.

---

## V.5 — Torah vs. instruction; "began to expound" vs. "undertook to expound" vs. "undertook to make plain"

- **EMET:** "Moshe began to expound this Torah"
- **LOGOS:** "Moses undertook to expound this Torah"
- **PNEUMA:** "Moses undertook to make plain this instruction"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Moses undertook to expound this instruction." "Undertook" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) better captures the volitional, formal character of *ho'il*. "Instruction" replaces the transliterated "Torah" per project style. "Expound" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "make plain" — *be'er* involves active digging-out, not merely simplifying.

---

## V.7 — "Euphrates" vs. "Perat"

- **EMET:** "the river Perat"
- **LOGOS:** "the River Euphrates"
- **PNEUMA:** "the Euphrates"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the Euphrates" (PNEUMA/LOGOS) — conventional English name per project convention.

---

## V.9 — "carry" vs. "bear"

- **EMET:** "I am not able to carry you alone"
- **LOGOS:** "I am not able alone to bear you"
- **PNEUMA:** "I cannot carry you on my own"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "I am not able to carry you alone" — "carry" (EMET/PNEUMA) is more faithful to *nasa'* (physical carrying). LOGOS's "bear" is acceptable but slightly less vivid.

---

## V.12 — third noun: "striving / strife / disputes and quarrels"

- **EMET:** "your burden and your load and your striving"
- **LOGOS:** "your burden and your load and your strife"
- **PNEUMA:** "the burden of you and your disputes and your quarrels"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "your burden and your load and your strife" (LOGOS). The Hebrew *rivkhem* is singular (your contention). PNEUMA's double rendering ("disputes and quarrels") over-expands. EMET's "striving" is slightly abstract. "Strife" is clear and concise.

---

## V.13 — third qualification: "knowing" vs. "experienced"

- **EMET:** "wise and discerning and knowing, for your tribes"
- **LOGOS:** "wise and discerning and experienced men, according to your tribes"
- **PNEUMA:** "wise, discerning, and experienced men from your tribes"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "experienced" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew *yeda'im* (participial of *yada'*) denotes men of practical, recognized competence — "experienced" captures this better than the abstract "knowing."

---

## V.16 — "sojourner" formulation

- **EMET:** "between a man and his brother and his sojourner"
- **LOGOS:** "between a man and his brother and the sojourner who is with him"
- **PNEUMA:** "between a fellow Israelite, and between the native and the sojourner"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "between a man and his brother and the sojourner who is with him" (LOGOS). The MT has *gero* (his sojourner), and LOGOS's fuller rendering is faithful. PNEUMA inserts "native" which is not in the Hebrew at this verse.

---

## V.17 — "partiality" / "fear" / "intimidated"

- **EMET:** "You shall not show partiality in judgment...you shall not fear any man's face"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall not be partial in judgment...You shall not be intimidated by anyone"
- **PNEUMA:** "You must not show partiality in judgment...Do not be intimidated by anyone"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "You shall not be partial in judgment...You shall not be intimidated by anyone" (LOGOS). "Partial" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is clearer than EMET's "show partiality." "Intimidated" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) captures *lo taguru mipney ish* better than EMET's literal "fear any man's face."

---

## V.19 — "terrifying" vs. "fearful"

- **EMET:** "great and fearful wilderness"
- **LOGOS:** "great and terrifying wilderness"
- **PNEUMA:** "that whole terrifying wilderness — the great and fearful desert"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "great and terrifying wilderness" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's double description over-translates. "Terrifying" is preferred over "fearful" for the physical danger context.

---

## V.27 — causal construction

- **EMET:** "In the LORD's hatred of us he has brought us out of the land of Mitsrayim"
- **LOGOS:** "Because the LORD hates us he brought us out of the land of Egypt"
- **PNEUMA:** "Because the LORD hates us he brought us out of Egypt"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Because the LORD hates us he brought us out of the land of Egypt" (LOGOS). The causal conjunction is more natural English than EMET's nominal construction. "Egypt" vs. "the land of Egypt": SOPHIA retains "the land of Egypt" to match v.30's parallel structure.

---

## V.30 — "did for you" vs. "did with you"

- **EMET:** "according to all that he did with you in Mitsrayim"
- **LOGOS:** "according to all that he did for you in Egypt"
- **PNEUMA:** "just as he did everything for you in Egypt"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "all that he did for you in Egypt" (LOGOS). The Hebrew *asah...lekha* is "did for you." PNEUMA's addition of "everything" is not in the MT.

---

## V.31 — "as a man carries his son" vs. "as a father carries his child"

- **EMET:** "as a man carries his son"
- **LOGOS:** "as a man carries his son"
- **PNEUMA:** "as a father carries his child"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "as a man carries his son" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew is *ka'asher yissa' ish et-beno* — a man, his son. "Father" (PNEUMA) is interpretively accurate but not in the text. "Son" preserves the gender-specific tenderness of the original.

---

## V.35 — negative oath construction

- **EMET:** "If any man of these men of this evil generation see the good land..."
- **LOGOS:** "Not one of these men, of this evil generation, shall see the good land..."
- **PNEUMA:** "Not a single person from this evil generation shall see the good land..."
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Not one of these men of this evil generation shall see the good land" (LOGOS). The declarative negative (LOGOS/PNEUMA) renders the Hebrew oath formula in natural English. EMET's conditional form preserves the literal grammatical form but is awkward in English. LOGOS is adopted with slight compression.

---

## V.37 — placement and phrasing of Moses' exclusion

- **EMET:** "Also against me the LORD showed anger on your account"
- **LOGOS:** "With me also the LORD was angry on your account"
- **PNEUMA:** "The LORD was even angry with me on your account"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "The LORD was angry with me also on your account" — closest to LOGOS. EMET's inversion is awkward; PNEUMA's "even" is slightly interpretive.

---

## V.40 — "Reed Sea" vs. "Red Sea"

- **EMET:** "the way of the Reed Sea"
- **LOGOS:** "the way of the Red Sea"
- **PNEUMA:** "the road to the Red Sea"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the way of the Red Sea" (LOGOS). The conventional English name "Red Sea" is used throughout the project. "Way" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "road" (PNEUMA) for the directional idiom *derekh*.

---

## V.41 — "girded" vs. "buckled on"

- **EMET:** "each man girded his weapons of war"
- **LOGOS:** "every man of you girded on his weapons of war"
- **PNEUMA:** "each of you buckled on his weapons"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "every man of you buckled on his weapons of war" — combining LOGOS's fuller subject with PNEUMA's more natural English verb, and retaining "of war" for precision.

---

## V.43 — "acted presumptuously" vs. "arrogantly marched"

- **EMET:** "you acted presumptuously and went up to the hill-country"
- **LOGOS:** "acted presumptuously and went up into the hill country"
- **PNEUMA:** "You defied the LORD's command and arrogantly marched up into the hill country"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "acted presumptuously and went up into the hill country" (LOGOS). "Presumptuously" renders *va-tazidu* precisely. PNEUMA adds "arrogantly marched" which conflates and expands.

---

## V.44 — bee simile

- **EMET:** "chased you as the bees do"
- **LOGOS:** "chased you as bees do"
- **PNEUMA:** "chased you like a swarm of bees"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "chased you as bees do" (LOGOS). "As bees do" faithfully renders *ka'asher ta'asenah ha-devorim*. PNEUMA's "swarm" is an addition not in the MT.

---

## V.45 — "did not hear" vs. "did not listen" / "did not give ear"

- **EMET:** "the LORD did not hear your voice and did not give ear to you"
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD did not listen to your voice or give ear to you"
- **PNEUMA:** "the LORD paid no attention to your weeping and gave you no hearing"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the LORD did not listen to your voice or give ear to you" (LOGOS). PNEUMA substitutes "weeping" for "voice" — the Hebrew is *qolkhem* (your voice). "Did not listen" and "did not give ear" are both present in the Hebrew; SOPHIA retains both.

---

## V.46 — "many days" repetition

- **EMET:** "you dwelt in Kadesh many days, as the days that you dwelt [there]"
- **LOGOS:** "you remained at Kadesh many days — the days that you remained there"
- **PNEUMA:** "So you stayed at Kadesh many days — the long days you remained there"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "you remained at Kadesh many days — the days that you remained there" (LOGOS). The deliberate Hebrew repetition (*rabbim yamim ka'yamim asher yeshavtem*) is preserved. PNEUMA's addition of "long" is not in the MT.
